title: Jaundice and transaminitis in the thousands in a polypharmacy patient after travel

chart.cc: Yellow eyes and feeling wiped out
chart.oneLiner: 31M with HIV (on a single-tablet regimen), latent TB on isoniazid, and hyperlipidemia on a statin presents with ten days of malaise, anorexia, nausea with vomiting, right upper quadrant discomfort, dark urine and now jaundice — after recent overseas travel, a new tattoo and a new partner abroad, several days of heavy over-the-counter acetaminophen for a febrile illness, and a new bodybuilding supplement.

opening: My eyes and skin have gone yellow, my pee is the color of cola, and I've been queasy and wiped out for a week and a half.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 31-year-old man, mildly jaundiced, tired and a little nauseated. Cooperative and a reasonable historian, but embarrassed about some of the exposures and about how much Tylenol he took, so he only gives those details when asked directly.

HPI: About ten days of malaise, poor appetite, nausea with a few episodes of vomiting, low-grade fevers, and a dull right upper quadrant ache. Over the last several days his urine turned dark (cola-colored), his stools went pale, and friends told him his eyes looked yellow. He has been eating and drinking very little and vomited several times, so he feels lightheaded when he stands. NO confusion, NO trouble staying awake, NO abnormal sleepiness, NO personality change (he would deny any altered behavior — that would suggest the liver failing). NO black/bloody stools, NO easy bruising or bleeding gums.
EXPOSURES (reveal ONLY when asked about travel, tattoos, sex, drug use, or medicines/supplements): Returned three weeks ago from a month overseas in a region with poor sanitation; ate street food and drank questionable water (Hepatitis A risk). While abroad he got a new tattoo at a street shop and had a new sexual partner with inconsistent condom use (Hepatitis B/C risk). He has never injected drugs. For a febrile flu-like illness on the trip he took a LOT of over-the-counter acetaminophen — he admits maybe 3 to 4 grams a day, sometimes more, for about five days, mixing a cold-and-flu combination product with extra Tylenol because he 'didn't think Tylenol could hurt you.' He also started a new mail-order 'liver-and-muscle' bodybuilding supplement stack about six weeks ago (green-tea extract plus an anabolic-sounding ingredient) to bulk up at the gym.
PMH/COMORBIDITIES (reveal when asked about medical history or medicines): HIV, diagnosed two years ago, well controlled, on a single-tablet regimen (bictegravir/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine), takes it daily, last CD4 and viral load were good. Latent TB found on screening, six months into isoniazid (with vitamin B6). Hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin. He has had muscle aches and dark-ish urine for a couple of weeks that he blamed on hard gym workouts.
MEDS: bictegravir/TAF/emtricitabine daily; isoniazid + pyridoxine daily; atorvastatin nightly; the new OTC bodybuilding supplement stack; and the heavy acetaminophen/cold-and-flu product over the past week. SOCIAL: social drinker, about 4-6 drinks on a weekend night, more while traveling; works in IT; goes to the gym. No injection drug use. FAMILY: noncontributory. ROS: as above — jaundice, dark urine, pale stool, nausea/vomiting, anorexia, myalgias; no confusion, no bleeding, no chest pain, no palpitations (though he sometimes feels his heart 'flutter' when standing).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: mildly jaundiced (scleral icterus), tired-appearing, dry-looking.
- Vitals: BP 108/68 (drops to 96/60 with a rise in heart rate on standing), HR 96, RR 16, 37.8°C, SpO2 98% RA. Mucous membranes dry.
- Abdomen: tender hepatomegaly in the RUQ; no splenomegaly; NO ascites/shifting dullness; no caput, no spider angiomata, no palmar erythema (no stigmata of CHRONIC liver disease).
- Neuro: alert and fully oriented; NO asterixis; no flap, no confusion (no encephalopathy).
- Skin/MSK: no track marks; mild diffuse proximal muscle tenderness (he has been lifting and on a statin).

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis or use textbook terms. Answer only what is asked, in plain language.
- Do NOT volunteer the travel, tattoo, sexual exposure, acetaminophen amount, the supplement stack, the HIV/INH/statin medicines, or the muscle aches unless asked about the matching topic (travel/exposures, sex, drug use, medications/supplements, past medical history, or muscle symptoms).
- Reveal exam findings (jaundice, RUQ tenderness, orthostatic vitals, dry membranes, absence of asterixis, muscle tenderness) ONLY when that maneuver is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.LFTs: AST 2100, ALT 2750 (ALT > AST, markedly elevated — a hepatocellular pattern far out of proportion to mild drug injury); Alk Phos 150 (mildly elevated); GGT mildly elevated; albumin 3.6.
labs.Bilirubin: Total bilirubin 7.8 mg/dL with a DIRECT (conjugated) predominance of 5.6 mg/dL (conjugated hyperbilirubinemia → cholestasis from hepatocellular injury, not hemolysis).
labs.Coags: INR 1.4 (mildly elevated — borderline synthetic dysfunction; a rising INR with any encephalopathy would define acute liver failure and change everything).
labs.Acetaminophen: Acetaminophen level 42 mcg/mL drawn ~16 h after his last large dose — DETECTABLE but BELOW the Rumack-Matthew treatment line for that time point; so APAP is a contributor/red herring, NOT the dominant cause, but staggered/chronic ingestion lowers the threshold to treat — give NAC if any doubt.
labs.BMP_renal: Na 134, K 3.0 (HYPOkalemia from vomiting and poor intake), HCO3 20, Cl 102; BUN 34, Cr 1.7 (baseline ~0.9 → acute kidney injury, prerenal from vomiting/poor intake with a possible nephrotoxic component); phosphate 2.0 and magnesium 1.5 (both low).
labs.CK: Creatine kinase 1400 U/L (mild rhabdomyolysis — statin plus a bodybuilding supplement plus heavy gym work — contributing to the AKI and to the dark urine, a SEPARATE problem from the liver).
labs.ViralSerologies: Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) POSITIVE and anti-HBc IgM POSITIVE → acute Hepatitis B; HBeAg positive (high infectivity). Hepatitis A IgM NEGATIVE; Hepatitis C antibody negative with HCV RNA pending; HIV viral load undetectable on therapy.
labs.US: RUQ ultrasound: hepatomegaly with a coarse but non-cirrhotic echotexture, patent portal/hepatic vessels, NO biliary ductal dilatation or stones (no obstruction), no ascites — argues against biliary obstruction and against established cirrhosis.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus rhythm ~96 bpm with the changes of HYPOKALEMIA: prominent U waves (best seen V2-V3), flattened/low-amplitude T waves, mild ST depression, and a long QU interval that mimics a prolonged QT. No acute ischemic ST-elevation. This is the patient's OWN tracing and reflects his K 3.0 / Mg 1.5 / phosphate 2.0.
ekg.verify: Source a tracing matching: sinus rhythm with prominent U waves, flat T waves, ST depression and apparent QT prolongation (hypokalemia) — self-host with credit to litfl.com.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead in sinus rhythm with prominent U waves, flattened T waves, mild ST depression and apparent QT prolongation — the electrocardiographic signature of hypokalemia.
searchTerms: hypokalemia ECG U waves flattened T waves
hypokalaemia 12 lead U wave ST depression long QU
ekg.clinicalVignette: A vomiting, poorly-eating patient with potassium 3.0 and low magnesium has a tracing showing prominent U waves and a long-appearing QT.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray (PA)
cxr.findings: Clear lung fields; no focal consolidation to suggest aspiration after his vomiting; no pleural effusion; no free air under the diaphragm; normal cardiomediastinal silhouette.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: clear lungs, no consolidation, no effusion, no subdiaphragmatic free air, normal heart size. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph with clear lungs, no consolidation, no effusion and no subdiaphragmatic free air
searchTerms: clear chest radiograph no consolidation no effusion
cxr.clinicalVignette: A nauseated, vomiting patient has a screening chest film to exclude aspiration after several days of vomiting.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, give your differential for transaminases in the thousands in this patient with HIV, isoniazid, a statin, heavy acetaminophen, a new supplement, and recent travel/tattoo/sexual exposure (note your leading diagnosis). Separately, name THREE distinct etiologies or mechanisms of acute hepatocellular injury you are weighing here.
steps.rubric: Acute viral hepatitis (leading,6). Drug-/toxin-induced liver injury — the genuine competitor here: acetaminophen, isoniazid, the statin, AND the bodybuilding/green-tea supplement are all hepatotoxins (CANT-MISS — must exclude/treat APAP empirically,5). Names THREE distinct etiologies/mechanisms of hepatocellular injury (e.g. viral, direct dose-dependent toxin like APAP, idiosyncratic drug reaction like INH/supplement, ischemic, autoimmune) (core,3). Alcoholic hepatitis given binge drinking — but AST:ALT here is >1 so less likely (core,2). Autoimmune hepatitis (core,1). Ischemic hepatitis given vomiting/orthostasis/hypotension (core,2). Biliary obstruction (core,1). Recognizes a markedly hepatocellular ALT>AST pattern AND that APAP/supplement/INH must be excluded/treated even while viral is leading (core,3). PENALTY: anchoring on acetaminophen toxicity alone and not sending viral serologies, OR anchoring on viral and ignoring the polypharmacy hepatotoxins (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute viral hepatitis (exposure history of travel, a new tattoo, and a new sexual partner; markedly hepatocellular ALT>AST in the thousands). The genuine competitor is drug-/toxin-induced liver injury, and it is layered: heavy acetaminophen, isoniazid, atorvastatin, and an OTC green-tea/bodybuilding supplement are ALL hepatotoxins — APAP must be excluded with a level and treated empirically if there is any doubt. Three distinct etiologies/mechanisms of acute hepatocellular injury in play: (1) viral infection; (2) direct dose-dependent toxin (acetaminophen); (3) idiosyncratic drug/supplement reaction (isoniazid, the herbal stack) — and one could add ischemic hepatitis given his vomiting/orthostatic hypotension. Also reasonable: alcoholic hepatitis (but AST:ALT >1 here makes it less likely), autoimmune hepatitis, and biliary obstruction.

criticalActions.item: Drug-/toxin-induced liver injury (acetaminophen, isoniazid, statin, or supplement)

coreActions.item: Names three distinct etiologies/mechanisms of hepatocellular injury
Recognizes markedly hepatocellular ALT>AST pattern and that APAP/supplement/INH must be excluded or treated even while viral leads

bonusActions.item: Notes the muscle aches plus dark urine could be a separate rhabdomyolysis (statin/supplement), not the liver

penalties.item: Anchors on acetaminophen alone without sending viral serologies, OR anchors on viral and ignores the polypharmacy hepatotoxins

mustNotMiss: Drug-/toxin-induced liver injury (acetaminophen, isoniazid, statin, or supplement)

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order? This patient is on multiple potentially hepatotoxic drugs, has been vomiting, and has muscle aches with dark urine.
steps.rubric: LFTs incl. AST/ALT pattern (CANT-MISS,3). ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing for the nomogram (CANT-MISS,3). Viral hepatitis serologies — A/B/C with HBsAg + anti-HBc IgM (3). Bilirubin (fractionated) (1). INR/coags — synthetic function (2). BMP/renal panel + Mg/phosphate (electrolytes, AKI) (2). CK/urinalysis for myoglobin (rhabdo) (1). RUQ ultrasound (1). EKG given the electrolyte derangement (1). PENALTY: not checking an acetaminophen level in acute hepatocellular injury, OR not sending viral serologies (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: LFTs with the AST/ALT pattern; an ACETAMINOPHEN level (note the timing relative to his last large/staggered dose for the Rumack-Matthew nomogram); fractionated bilirubin; INR/coags for synthetic function; viral hepatitis serologies (HAV IgM, HBsAg + anti-HBc IgM + HBeAg, HCV Ab/RNA) plus HIV viral load; a BMP/renal panel with magnesium and phosphate (he has been vomiting — expect hypokalemia and AKI); CK and a urinalysis for myoglobin given the muscle aches and dark urine (rhabdomyolysis from statin/supplement); RUQ ultrasound to exclude obstruction; and EKG + CXR.

criticalActions.item: ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing for the nomogram

coreActions.item: Viral serologies (HBsAg + anti-HBc IgM, HAV IgM, HCV)
BMP/renal panel with Mg and phosphate

penalties.item: Not checking an acetaminophen level in acute hepatocellular injury, or not sending viral serologies

requiredTests: ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing for the nomogram
contraindicatedTests: Not checking an acetaminophen level in acute hepatocellular injury, or not sending viral serologies
orderSensitiveActions: ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing for the nomogram

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is THIS patient's 12-lead EKG, taken in the ED. Describe your findings and tie them to his labs.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus rhythm with the changes of HYPOKALEMIA (with hypomagnesemia): prominent U waves, flattened/low T waves, ST depression, and an apparently long QT (really a long QU). CREDIT: systematic rate & rhythm read (4); recognizes U waves / flat T waves / ST depression as hypokalemia (6); links it to his measured K 3.0 / Mg 1.5 / phosphate 2.0 from vomiting and poor intake AND flags the long QT/QU as a torsades/arrhythmia risk requiring repletion (5). PENALTY: calling the study normal, OR reading the apparent QT prolongation without recognizing hypokalemia as the cause (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes hypokalemia changes (U waves, flat T waves, ST depression)
links the EKG to measured K 3.0 / Mg 1.5 / phosphate 2.0 and flags long QT/QU as arrhythmia risk
criticalFindings: Sinus rhythm with hypokalemia changes — prominent U waves, flattened T waves, ST depression and apparent QT prolongation — matching K 3.0 / low Mg, with torsades risk until repleted.
commonMistakes: reporting QT prolongation without recognizing hypokalemia as the cause
mistaking U waves for extra P waves or for T waves
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus rhythm at ~96 bpm with the electrocardiographic signature of hypokalemia: prominent U waves (best in V2-V3), flattened/low-amplitude T waves, mild ST depression, and an apparently prolonged QT that is really a long QU interval. This is the patient's OWN derangement — it matches his potassium of 3.0 with low magnesium (1.5) and phosphate (2.0) from days of vomiting and poor intake. The long QU/QT places him at risk for ventricular arrhythmia/torsades, so it mandates urgent potassium AND magnesium repletion with telemetry — a separate, concurrent problem from his hepatitis.

criticalActions: Sinus rhythm with hypokalemia changes — prominent U waves, flattened T waves, ST depression and apparent QT prolongation — matching K 3.0 / low Mg, with torsades risk until repleted.
coreActions: recognizes hypokalemia changes (U waves, flat T waves, ST depression)
links the EKG to measured K 3.0 / Mg 1.5 / phosphate 2.0 and flags long QT/QU as arrhythmia risk

penalties.label: calls the study normal, or reads the apparent QT prolongation without recognizing hypokalemia as the cause

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray, obtained because he has been vomiting for days. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear lungs with NO aspiration consolidation, NO pleural effusion, NO subdiaphragmatic free air, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette. CREDIT: recognizes a normal film (5); specifically states the pertinent negatives — no aspiration consolidation after vomiting and no free air (5). PENALTY: calling the study abnormal / inventing an infiltrate, or failing to state the deliberate pertinent negatives (-3).
keyFindings: clear lung fields with no aspiration consolidation
no pleural effusion and no subdiaphragmatic free air
criticalFindings: Clear chest film: no aspiration consolidation after vomiting, no effusion, no free air under the diaphragm.
commonMistakes: inventing an infiltrate on a clear film
failing to comment on the absence of aspiration or free air
steps.idealAnswer: Clear lung fields with a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette and — importantly given several days of vomiting — no focal consolidation to suggest aspiration, no pleural effusion, and no free air under the diaphragm.

criticalActions: Clear chest film: no aspiration consolidation after vomiting, no effusion, no free air under the diaphragm.
coreActions: clear lung fields with no aspiration consolidation
no pleural effusion and no subdiaphragmatic free air

penalties.label: calls the study abnormal / invents pathology

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: ALT>AST in the thousands, an acetaminophen level that is detectable but below the treatment line, HBsAg POSITIVE with anti-HBc IgM POSITIVE, plus a Cr of 1.7, K 3.0 and CK 1400, and an EKG showing hypokalemia. Commit to a diagnosis, name the discriminating finding, state any residual ambiguity, and list the separate concurrent problems you must manage.
steps.rubric: Acute viral hepatitis — acute Hepatitis B (HBsAg+ / anti-HBc IgM+) is the leading and now best-supported diagnosis (6). Names the discriminator: the markedly hepatocellular pattern with positive acute HBV serology, with an APAP level below the nomogram line — so APAP/INH/statin/supplement are contributors, not the dominant cause; states the residual ambiguity that staggered/chronic APAP lowers the threshold so empiric NAC is still reasonable and the other hepatotoxins should be held (4). Identifies the SEPARATE concurrent problems: AKI (prerenal from vomiting + rhabdo), hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia driving the EKG, and CK-1400 rhabdomyolysis from statin/supplement — not all one disease.
steps.idealAnswer: Commit to acute viral hepatitis — specifically acute Hepatitis B (HBsAg positive with anti-HBc IgM positive, HBeAg positive). The discriminating finding is the positive acute-HBV serology together with a markedly hepatocellular ALT>AST pattern in the thousands, while the acetaminophen level sits BELOW the Rumack-Matthew treatment line for the timing drawn — so acetaminophen, isoniazid, the statin and the herbal supplement are contributors and red herrings rather than the dominant cause. Residual ambiguity: a staggered/chronic acetaminophen ingestion lowers the threshold to treat, so empiric N-acetylcysteine is still reasonable if there is any doubt, and all the other hepatotoxic drugs (INH, statin, supplement) should be held. Crucially, this is NOT one disease: separate concurrent problems are an acute kidney injury (prerenal from vomiting/poor intake, with a nephrotoxic/myoglobin component), hypokalemia with hypomagnesemia and hypophosphatemia that explain the EKG and create torsades risk, and a rhabdomyolysis (CK 1400) from the statin plus bodybuilding supplement plus heavy lifting.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition? Decide whether this patient can be safely discharged or must be admitted, and justify it.
steps.rubric: Per MGH viral hepatitis: supportive care — IV fluids for the orthostatic AKI, antiemetics, rest, and STOP/avoid all hepatotoxins: hold acetaminophen, isoniazid, the statin and the supplement; counsel on alcohol (CANT-MISS,6). Give empiric N-acetylcysteine if any doubt about the staggered acetaminophen ingestion (4). Replete potassium AND magnesium (and phosphate) urgently with telemetry for the hypokalemia/long-QU on EKG; treat the rhabdomyolysis with fluids (4). Monitor INR and mental status for acute liver failure; hepatology/transplant referral if INR rises or encephalopathy develops; start/continue antiviral and involve ID given HIV + acute HBV (3). Counsel on transmission, vaccinate/serologically check contacts, and report to public health (2). Disposition: ADMIT — do NOT discharge: he has INR 1.4, AKI, symptomatic hypokalemia with EKG changes, rhabdomyolysis, and ongoing vomiting; arrange safe discharge LATER only with medication reconciliation (which hepatotoxins to stop), follow-up labs, and close hepatology/ID follow-up (CANT-MISS,4). PENALTY: discharging this patient home now (-4); continued hepatotoxins/alcohol (-3); missing progression to acute liver failure (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Admit — this patient should NOT be discharged now: he has a mildly elevated INR (1.4), an AKI (Cr 1.7), symptomatic hypokalemia with hypomagnesemia and EKG changes (torsades risk), a rhabdomyolysis, and ongoing vomiting. Supportive care: IV fluids to correct the prerenal AKI and protect the kidneys from myoglobin, antiemetics, and rest. STOP/avoid all hepatotoxins — hold the acetaminophen, isoniazid, the atorvastatin, and the bodybuilding supplement, and counsel strict alcohol avoidance. Give empiric N-acetylcysteine if there is any doubt about the staggered/chronic acetaminophen ingestion. Urgently replete potassium AND magnesium (you cannot fix the potassium without the magnesium) plus phosphate, on telemetry, to resolve the U waves and the long QU. Monitor the INR and mental status closely for acute liver failure and refer to hepatology/transplant if the INR rises or encephalopathy develops. Because he has HIV with acute Hepatitis B, involve Infectious Diseases and start/continue an HBV-active antiviral. Counsel on transmission, check/vaccinate close and sexual contacts, and report the acute hepatitis to public health. Arrange safe discharge only later, with explicit medication reconciliation (which hepatotoxic drugs to stop and which to resume), follow-up LFT/INR/renal labs, and close hepatology and ID follow-up.

criticalActions.item: Supportive care with IV fluids and STOP/avoid all hepatotoxins (hold APAP, INH, statin, supplement; counsel alcohol)
Admit and do not discharge now; arrange safe discharge later with med reconciliation and close hepatology/ID follow-up

coreActions.item: Empiric N-acetylcysteine if any doubt about staggered acetaminophen
Replete potassium AND magnesium (and phosphate) with telemetry; treat rhabdomyolysis with fluids
Monitor INR and mental status for ALF; hepatology/transplant referral if coagulopathy/encephalopathy; ID + HBV-active antiviral given HIV
Counsel on transmission and vaccinate/check contacts

penalties.item: Missing progression to acute liver failure

requiredActions: Supportive care with IV fluids and STOP/avoid all hepatotoxins (hold APAP, INH, statin, supplement; counsel alcohol)
Admit and do not discharge now; arrange safe discharge later with med reconciliation and close hepatology/ID follow-up
optionalActions: Empiric N-acetylcysteine if any doubt about staggered acetaminophen
Replete potassium AND magnesium (and phosphate) with telemetry; treat rhabdomyolysis with fluids
Monitor INR and mental status for ALF; hepatology/transplant referral if coagulopathy/encephalopathy; ID + HBV-active antiviral given HIV
Counsel on transmission and vaccinate/check contacts
unsafeActions: Missing progression to acute liver failure
steps.disposition: Admit (INR 1.4, AKI, symptomatic hypokalemia with EKG changes, rhabdomyolysis, vomiting); safe discharge only later with med reconciliation and close follow-up

physicalExamMappings.finding: Abdomen: tender hepatomegaly in the RUQ; no splenomegaly; no ascites; no stigmata of chronic liver disease.
Abdomen: no shifting dullness and no ascites (argues against decompensated chronic liver disease).
Neuro: alert and oriented; NO asterixis/encephalopathy (no current sign of acute liver failure).

Orthostatic vitals POSITIVE: BP drops from 108/68 to 96/60 with a compensatory rise in heart rate on standing, and mucous membranes are dry — volume depletion from vomiting and poor intake (explains the prerenal AKI).
MSK: mild diffuse proximal muscle tenderness; strength preserved (consistent with statin/supplement-related myopathy/rhabdomyolysis).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 31M with HIV (on a single-tablet regimen), latent TB on isoniazid, and hyperlipidemia on a statin presents with ten days of malaise, anorexia, vomiting, RUQ discomfort, dark urine and jaundice after overseas travel, a new tattoo, a new sexual partner, several days of heavy acetaminophen, and a new bodybuilding supplement. The diagnosis is Acute viral hepatitis (acute Hepatitis B: HBsAg+/anti-HBc IgM+), reached by separating a genuine viral-vs-drug/toxin fork: the markedly hepatocellular ALT>AST pattern with positive acute-HBV serology makes viral hepatitis the leading cause, while a subtoxic acetaminophen level, isoniazid, the statin and the supplement are contributors/red herrings to exclude or treat empirically. Concurrent, SEPARATE problems — a prerenal AKI, symptomatic hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia (driving the EKG), and a statin/supplement rhabdomyolysis — must each be recognized and managed.
keyHistoryClues: recent overseas travel with questionable food/water, a new tattoo, and a new sexual partner (viral hepatitis exposures)
several days of heavy over-the-counter acetaminophen plus a new green-tea/bodybuilding supplement, on top of isoniazid and a statin (layered hepatotoxins)
days of vomiting and poor intake with orthostatic symptoms; muscle aches and dark urine from gym work on a statin
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: tender hepatomegaly in the RUQ; no splenomegaly; no ascites; no stigmata of chronic liver disease.
Abdomen: no shifting dullness and no ascites (argues against decompensated chronic liver disease).
Neuro: alert and oriented; NO asterixis/encephalopathy (no current sign of acute liver failure).
Orthostatic vitals POSITIVE with dry mucous membranes — volume depletion explaining the prerenal AKI.
MSK: mild diffuse proximal muscle tenderness consistent with statin/supplement myopathy/rhabdomyolysis.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals, noting the polypharmacy and travel/exposure history.
Elicit the focused history, drawing out the sensitive exposures (travel, tattoo, sex), the true acetaminophen amount and the supplement, and the full medication list only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam — RUQ tenderness, jaundice, absence of asterixis, orthostatic vitals, and muscle tenderness.
Build a differential with a genuine viral-vs-drug/toxin fork and name three mechanisms of hepatocellular injury.
Order a focused workup (AST/ALT pattern, timed acetaminophen level, viral serologies, renal/electrolytes/Mg/phos, CK/UA) and read the patient's OWN hypokalemia EKG plus a clear CXR.

Commit to acute viral hepatitis (acute HBV), name the discriminator and residual APAP ambiguity, and separately manage the AKI, hypokalemia, and rhabdomyolysis — admitting the patient and deferring safe discharge to med reconciliation and close follow-up.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on acetaminophen toxicity and never sending viral serologies
Anchoring on viral hepatitis and ignoring the layered hepatotoxins (APAP/INH/statin/supplement)
Reading the EKG QT prolongation without recognizing hypokalemia
Discharging a patient with INR 1.4, AKI, symptomatic hypokalemia, and rhabdomyolysis
Missing progression to acute liver failure
managementPearls: Stop ALL hepatotoxins (acetaminophen, isoniazid, statin, supplement) and give empiric NAC if any doubt about staggered acetaminophen
Replete potassium AND magnesium together on telemetry; you cannot correct the potassium without the magnesium
criticalWorkupItems: ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing for the nomogram
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus rhythm with hypokalemia changes — prominent U waves, flattened T waves, ST depression and apparent QT prolongation — matching K 3.0 / low Mg, with torsades risk until repleted.
Clear chest film: no aspiration consolidation after vomiting, no effusion, no free air under the diaphragm.
criticalManagementItems: Supportive care with IV fluids and STOP/avoid all hepatotoxins (hold APAP, INH, statin, supplement; counsel alcohol)
Admit and do not discharge now; arrange safe discharge later with med reconciliation and close hepatology/ID follow-up
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus rhythm with hypokalemia changes (U waves, flat T waves, ST depression, apparent QT prolongation).
Clear chest film with no aspiration consolidation, effusion, or free air.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus rhythm with hypokalemia changes — prominent U waves, flattened T waves, ST depression and apparent QT prolongation — matching K 3.0 / low Mg, with torsades risk until repleted.
Clear chest film: no aspiration consolidation after vomiting, no effusion, no free air under the diaphragm.
imageInterpretationPearls: When the EKG looks like a long QT, check the potassium and magnesium — U waves and a long QU are the signature of hypokalemia and a treatable torsades risk.
An EKG can be the patient's OWN concurrent problem, not a decoupled drill — integrate it with the labs.
On a vomiting patient's chest film, the deliberate pertinent negatives are no aspiration and no free air.

items.item: Shows empathy and avoids shaming around alcohol, sexual exposure, and acetaminophen/supplement use
Asks permission before sensitive sexual/substance/HIV questions
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
